{Reading a Remote Teammate's Body: Heart Rate and Variability on the Avatar to Support Coordination}

%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Networked multiplayer games are among the most inhabited yet distributed interactive spaces: teammates act together while sitting far apart, so bodily signals that colocated players read at a glance never cross the network. We present a method for closing that gap: translating Science and Technology Studies commitments, that measurement constitutes rather than reports what it names, that classification carries consequences, and that any reading of a body is situated \cite{nelson_meeting_1996, bowker_sorting_2008, haraway_situated_1988}, into a wearable interface layer that renders heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV) as an animated halo around each player's avatar, compositionally encoding two separable channels (pulse and brightness for activation, ring coherence for variability) while withholding emotion words. We report two studies. Study 1 tested the icon's legibility online with a chance-referenced forced-choice design: 36 participants read HR at 66 percent and HRV at 58 percent accuracy from animation alone, well above the 25 percent expected by chance, with interpretations ordered as designed and workload on the low side of the scale. Study 2 implemented the acquisition, classification, and rendering architecture Study 1 left as a proposed design, and evaluated it during live cooperative play: 12 three-player teams (36 participants) completed an Overcooked-style task under a no-cue baseline, a numeric HR/HRV display, and the animated cue. Teams supported a struggling teammate 3.9 seconds faster under the Icon Cue than under No Cue, matching an explicit numeric display on speed and awareness while rating it significantly less invasive and no more effortful, though the numeric display was rated more useful for coordination and more trustworthy, a trade-off participants named directly. We contribute the STS-to-visual-encoding method itself, a compositional two-channel encoding and a chance-referenced legibility test as secondary novelties, and converging evidence that ambient bodily cues steady coordination across a distributed interactive space at a lower privacy cost than explicit disclosure.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}
Networked multiplayer games have become one of the largest settings where people cooperate with others they cannot see. Inside a single match, strangers and friends assemble into teams, divide roles, and steer shared outcomes through fast rounds of coordination. What holds such teams together is communication, and its quality tracks how well they perform: analyses of large player populations show that the volume and structure of team communication separate strong clans from weak ones \cite{bisberg_communication_2025}, and even a channel as thin as a map ping measurably shifts the result of a competitive match \cite{leavitt_ping_2016}. Coordination in these spaces rests on a stream of small signals that tell each member what the others attend to and how hard they are working.

One signal that teammates in a shared room read without effort never survives the trip across the network. When people occupy the same space, they register a partner's quickened breath, restless posture, and rising tension continuously and prereflectively, and they fold that reading into how they speak and act \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}. Distributed play strips this bodily layer away. A remote partner must infer effort, composure, and withdrawal from text, voice, and avatar motion, which carry far less than shared physical presence does. Controlled comparison confirms the cost: when the cues available in face to face contact are removed, collaborators understand one another less well, and judge one another more harshly, than when a representation of the partner's bodily state is restored \cite{tan_role_2014}.

The consequence is a specific failure of interpretation rather than a general lack of information. A teammate who has gone quiet may be concentrating, overloaded, or withdrawing, and the three call for opposite responses. Absent any read on the body, players guess, and wrong guesses accumulate into misattributed blame, mistimed help, and coordination that unravels under pressure. The problem this paper addresses is how to return a usable trace of that missing bodily layer to distributed teammates.

Sending physiological data across a network is not new. Heart rate has been carried into chat to raise empathy and awareness \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, into shared displays to deepen social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, and into cooperative and virtual games to strengthen the felt bond between players \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}. Two limits recur. First, a long affective computing tradition treats a biosignal as evidence for an underlying emotion to be detected and named \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, fixing a single interpretation onto a body whose signals are notoriously ambiguous. Second, studies that report warmer feelings between partners seldom test whether a viewer can actually read the state on display and decide what to do about it, or whether the representation communicates anything once its emotion labels are removed. Legibility, and the situated judgment that follows from it, stays largely unmeasured.

We take a different route, and its shape comes from Science and Technology Studies. If measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears merely to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, if the categories an interface imposes carry consequences of their own \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and if every reading of a body belongs to a particular viewer and moment \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, then an interface that stamps a teammate's heartbeat with the word calm or panic overreaches. Our display withholds such verdicts. It renders Apple Watch heart rate as pulse rate and brightness, standing for cardiovascular activation, and heart rate variability as the coherence and steadiness of a ring, standing for autonomic regulation \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}, shown as a halo around each teammate's avatar (Figure~\ref{fig:teaser}). These become graded, deliberately open cues that a teammate weighs against the unfolding match rather than reads off as fact \cite{suchman_plans_1987, wakkary_things_2021}. The artifact is built to make an argument, not a diagnosis.

We evaluated whether that argument is even readable. In an online study, 36 distributed participants viewed all sixteen combinations of four heart rate states and four variability states and reported what each conveyed, through established instruments for symbol comprehension, affective rating, and workload. Participants recovered heart rate at 66 percent and variability at 58 percent from animation alone, well past the 25 percent expected from guessing. Their interpretations were ordered as the design intended: perceived urgency and arousal rose with activation, while falling variability coherence lowered perceived stability and raised the intention to offer support, none of it routed through emotion words. Reading the display stayed on the low side of the workload scale, most participants would accept it in cooperative play, and several drew boundaries around exposing bodily data to teammates, a concern we take up directly.

That first study answers whether the icon is readable in isolation; it does not answer whether reading it changes what teammates do together. We therefore conducted a second study that carries the validated icon system out of the single-avatar browser task and into live cooperative play. Study 2 implements the acquisition, baseline-relative classification, and avatar-rendering architecture that Study 1 proposed but left unbuilt, embeds it in an Overcooked-style \cite{carroll_utility_2019, baek_toward_2022} cooperative cooking task, and evaluates it against a no-cue baseline and an explicit numeric HR/HRV display across scripted teammate-pressure events, so that the legibility question of Study 1 is followed by a question about awareness, support timing, coordination, workload, and privacy during actual play. Twelve three-player teams completed all three conditions; teams supported a struggling teammate 3.9 seconds faster under the Icon Cue than under No Cue, matching the Numeric Cue's speed while being rated significantly less invasive and no more effortful than playing with no cue at all, though the Numeric Cue was rated somewhat more useful for coordination and more trustworthy. Sections~\ref{sec:study2-methods} and~\ref{sec:study2-results} report this second study's methodology, implemented apparatus, and results in full.

We contribute:
\begin{itemize}
\item a design method that translates Science and Technology Studies commitments, that measurement constitutes rather than reports what it names, that classification carries consequences, and that any reading of a body is situated, into concrete visual encoding choices, producing a representation that offers social cues without assigning emotions;
\item a peripheral avatar display that instantiates that method for wrist worn heart rate and variability, designed for the shared interactive space of a multiplayer match, compositionally encoding the two signals as separable visual channels, pulse and brightness for activation, ring coherence for variability, rather than transmitting heart rate alone as almost all prior shared-physiology systems do;
\item empirical evidence, from an online legibility study that tests the display with a chance-referenced forced-choice design against a fixed accuracy criterion, rather than the open-ended interpretation typical of prior ambiguous-display work, that this label free representation is legible, interpreted in a graded and consistent way, and light enough in workload to be usable, together with the privacy limits players place around it;
\item an implemented, live-multiplayer instantiation of the proposed architecture, embedded in a custom Overcooked-style cooperative task and evaluated under a no-cue, numeric-cue, and icon-cue condition against scripted teammate-pressure events, extending the legibility question of Study 1 into a test of coordination behavior during actual cooperative play; and
\item evidence from that live test that the ambient cue speeds teammate support as much as an explicit numeric display while costing less workload and less perceived invasiveness, alongside a legibility-versus-precision trade-off on rated coordination usefulness and trust that qualifies the case for ambiguity as a design resource.
\end{itemize}

\section{Literature Review}

\subsection{Communication and Coordination in Distributed Play}
Multiplayer games reward study less as game systems than as places where scattered groups organize themselves. Communication volume and structure separate skilled teams from weak ones across large player populations \cite{bisberg_communication_2025}, and a signal as spare as a positional ping alters who wins a competitive round \cite{leavitt_ping_2016}. The channels behind these findings are deliberate and symbolic: messages, calls, pings. The involuntary bodily signals that regulate teamwork in a shared room, the ones a partner registers before either speaks \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, reach the game through no channel at all. This section traces how prior work has tried to carry the body across a network, and where each attempt stops short of the reading a teammate needs.

\subsection{Reading Physiology as Emotion}
One tradition treats the body as a store of emotion to be recovered by computation. Affective computing set the agenda of sensing a signal and inferring the feeling behind it \cite{picard_affective_1997}, and play research applied it directly, mapping cardiac, muscular, and electrodermal activity onto models of a player's emotional state \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007, ravaja_phasic_2006} and adjusting game difficulty from those readings \cite{nacke_biofeedback_2011}, a lineage now broad enough to have its own systematic reviews \cite{navarro_biofeedback_2021}. Surveys record both the reach of this approach and its persistent snag \cite{gan_human-computer_2022, robinson_lets_2020}: one elevated heart rate accompanies fear, exertion, and delight alike, so a system that prints a single emotion word onto it throws away the ambiguity the body genuinely carries. That interpretive move is the one our design declines. A related strand of play research uses heart rate not to name a feeling but to drive mechanics directly, for instance rewarding elevated exertion with in game power ups \cite{ketcheson_designing_2015}; this treats the signal as a control input rather than a cue for a teammate to interpret, which is the reading problem our work addresses instead.

\subsection{Sharing Physiology Between People}
A second line sends the signal to another person rather than to a classifier. Heart rate has been shared to build social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, folded into mobile chat to lift empathy and awareness \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, and visualized during remote collaboration, where it raised group cohesion and perceived warmth over voice alone \cite{tan_role_2014}. In play, coupled and visualized biosignals have deepened the bond between partners in cooperative and virtual settings \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}, consistent with evidence that physiological alignment tracks social closeness \cite{dikker_brain--brain_2017}. Shared cardiac signals have also entered more adversarial play, where visible biosignals become a strategic resource that players read to infer a hidden role in a social deception game \cite{fang_cardiac_2022}, which extends the sharing tradition beyond cooperation but leaves the same gap: readers act on the signal without a check that they identify it correctly. This work shows convincingly that a shared bodily signal changes how people relate. It leaves two things untested. It seldom asks whether a viewer can name the state on show, as distinct from feeling closer to its owner, and it seldom removes the emotional framing to check that the representation still communicates. Legibility, the precondition for acting on a cue mid match, is rarely what these studies measure.

\subsection{Situated Interpretation and a Science and Technology Studies Stance}
Where the first tradition locates meaning in the signal and the second in the bond, a third locates it in the moment of use. Situated and embodied accounts hold that action is read against an unfolding context rather than decoded from a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, and design research treats an artifact as a way to pose that question rather than close it \cite{wakkary_things_2021}. Science and Technology Studies sharpens the stakes for anything that measures a body: instruments help produce the phenomena they seem only to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, classifications carry consequences for those they sort \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and no reading of a body is a view from nowhere \cite{haraway_situated_1988}. Together these commitments argue against an interface that resolves a teammate's heartbeat into a verdict, and for one that offers a graded, open cue to be interpreted in place.

\subsection{Positioning the Present Work}
The closest prior art is Howell et al.'s ambiguous social display of skin conductance \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}, which already withholds emotion labels and treats interpretive openness as a design resource, the same design move this paper makes. We differ from it, and from the wider literature, in three specific ways rather than in the ambiguity commitment itself. First, where shared physiology systems, Howell et al.'s included, almost always transmit a single signal, we add heart rate variability as a second, separately mapped visual dimension, because the two indices carry different information: rate reflects momentary activation, while variability reflects the autonomic regulation underneath it \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}. Second, where ambiguous displays are typically evaluated through open-ended qualitative interpretation, we additionally quantify legibility with a forced-choice identification accuracy tested against a fixed chance level, which lets us report how much of the intended state a label free display still communicates rather than only whether it prompts interpretation. Third, where earlier displays are judged by the connection they produce, we judge ours by the reading it supports, testing whether a label free representation is legible before asking what it does socially. We do not claim ambiguity itself as new; we claim a compositional two-channel encoding and a chance-referenced legibility test as the specific extensions this paper adds to that line of work. Table~\ref{tab:related} sets the contrast out in full.

\begin{table*}[t]
\footnotesize
\caption{Systems that carry physiological data between people, and how the present work differs. \emph{Emotion labels} marks whether the display names a feeling (Named), shows a raw or connection oriented signal that implies one (Implicit), or names none (None). \emph{Reader legibility} marks whether the study tested that a viewer can identify the intended state from the display itself.}
\label{tab:related}
\begin{tabular}{@{}l p{2.4cm} p{3.2cm} p{1.3cm} p{1.6cm}@{}}
\toprule
Work & Signal and setting & Primary aim & Emotion labels & Reader legibility tested \\
\midrule
Picard \cite{picard_affective_1997} & Multiple signals, no shared display & Recognize and model affect by computation & Named & No \\
Mandryk and Atkins \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007} & Cardiac, muscular, electrodermal in play & Infer a player's emotion from physiology & Named & No \\
Slov\'ak et al. \cite{slovak_understanding_2012} & Heart rate, shared display & Support social connection & Implicit & No \\

Moge et al. \cite{moge_shared_2022} & Heart rate, cooperative game & Enrich shared play & Implicit & No \\

Howell et al. \cite{howell_biosignals_2016} & Skin conductance, ambiguous shared display & Explore ambiguity and emotional interpretation of one biosignal & None & Partial (qualitative interpretation, not forced-choice accuracy) \\

\section{Study 1 Methodology: Icon Legibility Validation}
\label{sec:study1-methods}
\noindent
This paper reports two studies. Study 1, described in this section, used a research through design methodology with two phases: in the design phase, we derived the icon system from Science and Technology Studies and built the single-avatar browser stimuli that render it; in the evaluation phase, we tested the resulting visual language for legibility and interpretation in a controlled online study. Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) then implements the same icon system as a live multiplayer prototype and evaluates it during cooperative play. The subsections below state Study 1's research questions and hypotheses, then describe the design rationale, the icon system that stood as the study material, the study design, the participants, the apparatus, the procedure, the measures, and the analysis, before closing with the proposed system architecture that Study 2 goes on to implement.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
We addressed three research questions.
\begin{description}
    \item[RQ1] Can players identify a teammate's heart rate state and heart rate variability state from the animated icon alone, without numbers or emotion words?
    \item[RQ2] Do the two visual channels carry the graded social meaning they were designed to carry, that is, does activation read as urgency and arousal, and does declining ring coherence read as lower stability and a stronger call for support?
    \item[RQ3] Is the display clear and light enough in workload to be usable during cooperative play?
\end{description}

We set five hypotheses.
\begin{description}
    \item[H1] Identification accuracy for both the heart rate state and the variability state exceeds the 25 percent chance level of a choice among four options.
    \item[H2] Perceived urgency and arousal rise monotonically with the heart rate state.
    \item[H3] Perceived stability falls as ring coherence declines across the variability states.
    \item[H4] The intention to offer support rises as ring coherence declines.
    \item[H5] The display registers low workload on the Task Load Index while returning high clarity and coordination usefulness.
\end{description}

\subsection{Design Rationale}
Rendering a teammate's cardiovascular state at all presumes that state is worth showing. Interoception, the continuous and largely prereflective sense of one's own physiological condition, is how a person normally accesses that information about themselves \cite{craig_how_2002}; the display's task is to give a second party partial access to a process the wearer otherwise senses alone. Three commitments from Science and Technology Studies then fixed how we did that. Because measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, we classified heart rate and variability relative to each player's own baseline rather than against an absolute clinical scale. Because the categories an interface installs carry consequences and harden into infrastructure \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, we used a small set of relative visual states and no named emotion. Because any reading of a body is situated and partial \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, and meaning forms in place rather than inside a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, we left the cues open for interpretation against the surrounding play. This treatment of ambiguity as a resource follows prior evidence that one bodily signal supports many readings depending on its context \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}. We pursued the design itself as inquiry through research creation \cite{amos_kyriaki_2015} and speculative design \cite{wakkary_things_2021}, in a tradition that treats the body as a resource for interface design in its own right rather than only a source of data to be extracted \cite{dauden_roquet_body_2020}.

\subsection{Icon System and Stimuli}
The stimulus set was the icon system itself. Heart rate, an index of momentary cardiovascular activation, controlled pulse speed, glow, and halo scale. Heart rate variability, a marker of autonomic regulation estimated from the interval between beats \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007, shaffer_overview_2017}, controlled the coherence of a surrounding ring, from a smooth continuous halo to a broken and jittering one. Each channel held four states defined relative to baseline, producing 16 combinations through the crossing of four heart rate states and four variability states. We rendered each state as a looping animation of five to seven seconds around a single avatar. Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, a participant who had learned each channel could read a combination not seen before. No state carried an emotion word.

Table~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} defines the four heart-rate states used as prototype design states rather than medical diagnoses.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Heart-rate stimulus classification used in the prototype. Thresholds were defined relative to baseline and were used to structure the simulated visual states rather than to provide medical or emotional diagnoses.}
\label{tab:hr-stimulus-classification}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.19\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{HR state} &
\textbf{Physiological meaning} &
\textbf{Prototype threshold} &
\textbf{Icon effect} \\
\midrule
\textbf{HR1: Resting / Low Activation} &
Calm or low cardiovascular activation &
Baseline to $+5$ bpm, or $\leq 10\%$ above baseline &
Slow halo pulse and soft glow \\
\textbf{HR2: Focused Activation} &
Engaged, alert, and mildly activated &
$+6$ to $+15$ bpm, or $11$ to $25\%$ above baseline &
Medium pulse and clearer glow \\
\textbf{HR3: High Activation} &
Intense engagement, pressure, or effort &
$+16$ to $+30$ bpm, or $26$ to $45\%$ above baseline &
Fast pulse and stronger glow \\
\textbf{HR4: Overdrive Activation} &
Very high activation and possible overload &
More than $+30$ bpm, or more than $45\%$ above baseline &
Rapid pulse, sharp glow, and stronger expansion \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

Table~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification} defines the four HRV states, expressed as percentages of baseline HRV using an RMSSD-style design logic, representing changes in coherence and fragmentation rather than fixed clinical states.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Heart-rate-variability stimulus classification used in the prototype. Thresholds were defined relative to baseline HRV and were used to structure the simulated visual states rather than to assign clinical or emotional labels.}
\label{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.20\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{HRV state} &
\textbf{Physiological meaning} &
\textbf{Prototype threshold} &
\textbf{Icon effect} \\
\midrule
\textbf{V1: High Coherence} &
High variability and flexible regulation &
$\geq 110\%$ of baseline HRV &
Smooth continuous ring, wide breathing halo, and stable motion \\
\textbf{V2: Stable Variability} &
Normal or baseline variability &
$90$ to $109\%$ of baseline HRV &
Clean dotted ring and regular halo motion \\
\textbf{V3: Reduced Variability} &
Lower flexibility and possible physiological strain &
$60$ to $89\%$ of baseline HRV &
Denser segmented ring with a slight wobble \\
\textbf{V4: Suppressed / Fragmented Variability} &
Very low variability and reduced regulatory stability &
Less than $60\%$ of baseline HRV &
Broken ring, visible jitter, and irregular opacity \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

Tables~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} and~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification} summarize the baseline-relative HR and HRV classifications used to generate the sixteen animated stimulus conditions. We label the four heart rate states HR1 to HR4 and the four variability states V1 to V4 throughout; these condition labels are distinct from the H1 to H5 hypotheses stated in Section~3.1.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Icon.jpg}
  \caption{The icon design evolves through theoretical perspectives from Science and Technology Studies (STS), transforming an abstract sensing field into a human avatar surrounded by a dynamic halo. The final icon visualizes physiological signals as an ambiguous social cue, where halo brightness, pulse, density, and stability vary with heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV).}
  \Description{A visual design figure showing variations in avatar shapes and layered halo structures used to represent changes in heart rate and heart-rate variability.}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Study Design}
We used a within participants design in which every participant viewed all 16 states, removing between person variation from the central comparisons. This format, in which each viewer judges every icon, matches recent within participants recognition tests of icon sets in HCI \cite{schafer_shape_2023}, and evaluating a heart rate driven display through a dedicated player facing study follows recent work on biofeedback games \cite{tu_biofeedback_2025}. We administered it online in two phases. The open phase presented 4 states per participant with all labels hidden, and the structured phase presented all 16 states in randomised order. Each participant therefore completed 20 rating trials, yielding 144 trials in the open phase and 576 in the structured phase across the sample, followed by one closing questionnaire per participant. The design deliberately tests absolute legibility against a fixed chance level rather than comparative legibility against an alternative encoding, following the convention of ISO 9186 symbol-comprehension testing, where a candidate symbol is evaluated against a comprehension criterion rather than against competing symbol designs \cite{iso_9186_2014}. We did not include a numeric or emotion-labeled comparison arm; Section~\ref{sec:limitations} returns to what that omission does and does not let us claim, and Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods} reports a follow-up study designed to make exactly that comparison.

\subsection{Participants}
We recruited thirty six adults through the author's university subject pool and personal network; participation was voluntary and uncompensated, and all recruited participants completed the study. Twenty one were aged 18 to 24, twelve were aged 25 to 34, two were aged 35 to 44, and one was aged 45 to 54. Gaming background covered four reported levels: 15 regular players, 10 frequent players, 9 casual players, and 2 expert or competitive players. Familiarity with heart rate and variability was not familiar for 4 participants, slight for 13, moderate for 15, and high for 4. For prior use of wearable devices, 14 participants reported rare use, 7 sometimes, 8 often, and 2 very often, and 5 did not report it. For visual accessibility, 31 reported no known issue, 3 reported motion sensitivity, and 2 reported a colour vision difficulty. Participants took part on a desktop or laptop (27), a phone (6), or a tablet (3). We did not collect gender. Table~\ref{tab:participant-demographics} summarises age and device use.

\subsection{Apparatus and Materials}
We ran the study in a standard web browser and generated each state with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, presenting one avatar inside a simulated team status panel. The heart rate and variability states were synthetic and produced the same animation logic the deployed interface would use, which isolated the reading of the display from the demands of live play and gave every participant an identical stimulus set. We did not require live capture at this stage, since streaming a wearable's heart rate into a game engine is already established \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}.

\subsection{Procedure}
Each session opened with consent and a short demographic form, then moved through the two phases. In the open phase, and with every label hidden, participants viewed a state, wrote in free text what it conveyed, rated their confidence, selected the closest option from a set of plain descriptions, and rated perceived activation and perceived stability on seven point scales. This followed the open logic of graphical symbol comprehension, where an unprompted answer reveals what a symbol communicates without text \cite{iso_9186_2014}. In the structured phase, participants saw all 16 states in randomised order and, for each, identified the heart rate state from four options and the variability state from four options, rated clarity, urgency, perceived stability, usefulness for coordination, and intention to offer support on seven point scales, and completed a seven point pictorial affective rating. We closed each session with a questionnaire that recorded workload, confidence, visual comfort, and five open responses covering the most helpful feature, the most confusing states, whether the display felt useful or invasive, willingness to use it in cooperative play, and suggested improvements.

\subsection{Ethics Note for Reviewers}
All participants gave informed consent before beginning the session, were told they could withdraw at any time without penalty, and were assigned an anonymous participant identifier rather than any directly identifying label. No directly identifying information was collected; demographic categories are reported only in aggregate (Table~\ref{tab:participant-demographics}).

\subsection{Measures}
We measured legibility as identification accuracy for the heart rate state and the variability state in the structured phase, each scored against the 25 percent expected from selecting among four options, following the forced choice recognition format used in recent icon studies \cite{schafer_shape_2023}. We assessed affective interpretation with the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM), a nonverbal pictorial scale for valence, arousal, and dominance that avoids emotion vocabulary \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, administered on seven points. We assessed workload with three items of the NASA Task Load Index, namely mental demand, effort, and frustration \cite{hart_development_1988}, on seven point scales. Five further seven point items captured the social reading of each state, including clarity, urgency, perceived stability, usefulness for coordination, and intention to offer support, and the open phase added a free text interpretation and a forced match to a plain description.

\subsection{Analysis}
We fixed the sample at 36 participants to match comparable within-subjects icon-comprehension studies in HCI \cite{schafer_shape_2023}. A sensitivity analysis for a two-sided one-sample $t$ test at $\alpha=.05$ indicates this sample gives at least 99 percent power to detect a large standardized effect ($d_z \geq 0.8$) and approximately 83 percent power for a medium effect ($d_z = 0.5$), so the study was powered to detect effects at or below the size of the smallest effect we report (Table~\ref{tab:legibility-tests}). For each legibility comparison, every participant contributed a single accuracy proportion, and we tested that proportion against the relevant chance level with a two sided one sample t test, reporting Cohen's $d_z$ alongside each test as the standardized mean deviation from chance. The four legibility comparisons address logically distinct, individually pre-specified questions under H1 (an unaided open-phase check, HR identification, HRV identification, and joint identification), so we treated each as its own confirmatory test rather than pooling them into one family; even under a conservative Bonferroni correction across all four ($\alpha = .0125$), every comparison remained significant. We tested the intended perceptual mappings with planned within participants linear trend analyses. For each outcome, every participant contributed a single linear trend slope across the ordered states, and we tested the slopes against zero with two sided one sample t tests, Holm corrected across the five planned tests. We summarised workload and the seven point rating items with descriptive statistics. We analysed the free text interpretations and the five open responses with reflexive thematic analysis \cite{braun_using_2006}, applied in line with current guidance on thematic analysis practice in HCI \cite{bowman_thematic_2023}, which produced the account of how participants read the states and the boundaries they placed on physiological visibility.

\subsection{Proposed System Architecture}
What we built and evaluated for Study 1 is the single-avatar icon-visualization layer described in Apparatus and Materials above, driven by scripted rather than live physiological input. The acquisition and classification layers needed to drive that layer from a real wearable, a photoplethysmography sensor tracking heart rate and heart rate variability, and a processing layer that classifies these measurements relative to each player's own resting baseline rather than an absolute clinical scale \cite{haraway_situated_1988, bowker_sorting_2008}, were at the time of Study 1 a proposed rather than a built architecture; Tables~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} and~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification} give the four-state classification this layer used for each channel. Each avatar's halo was designed to be driven by only two small state values, heart rate state and variability state, rather than a raw physiological time series, so that propagating it across a multiplayer session would require replicating only those two values per player rather than streaming continuous biometric data between clients. Feeding a live wearable feed into this architecture requires a streaming bridge between device and game client, a capability prior work shows is practical \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}.

Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) implements this acquisition, classification, and avatar-rendering pipeline as a working multiplayer prototype and evaluates it during live cooperative play; its apparatus subsection reports what was actually built and how it embeds the same baseline-relative HR/HRV classification used here.

\section{Study 1 Results}
\label{sec:study1-results}
Thirty six participants produced 576 recognition trials in the structured phase and 144 free interpretations in the open phase. Each participant contributed one linear trend slope per outcome, and we tested each slope against zero with a two sided one sample t test, with Holm correction across the five planned tests. Figure~\ref{fig:procedure} summarises the procedure. We report legibility, then the intended perceptual mappings, then performance across the full grid, then usability and workload, then the qualitative findings.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_3_Study_Procedure.png}
  \caption{The two-phase online procedure: an open phase (Phase A) presenting 4 unlabeled anchor states per participant, followed by a structured phase (Phase B) presenting all 16 states in randomised order, bracketed by consent/demographics, a training legend, and a closing questionnaire.}
  \Description{A diagram of the study procedure, showing consent and demographics, the open Phase A with four unlabeled states, a training legend, the structured Phase B with all sixteen states, and a closing questionnaire.}
  \label{fig:procedure}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Icon Legibility (H1)}
\label{sec:study1-legibility}
Before any label was shown, participants' best-match interpretations of the four unaided anchor states in the open phase averaged 46.5 percent correct against the 20 percent chance level for the five-option match (95 percent CI 37.0 to 56.0), $t(35)=5.66$, $p<.001$, indicating that the design read above chance even without prior exposure to the full state set, though well short of ceiling. In the structured phase, participants identified the heart rate state on 66.3 percent of trials (95 percent CI 58.5 to 74.2), $t(35)=10.66$, $p<.001$, and the variability state on 58.2 percent (95 percent CI 51.0 to 65.3), $t(35)=9.42$, $p<.001$, both far above the 25 percent expected from a choice among four options, supporting H1. Accuracy held across the range of states rather than collapsing at any one level. Participants recognised heart rate states at 68.8, 64.6, 64.6, and 67.4 percent from HR1 to HR4, and variability states at 64.6, 49.3, 55.6, and 63.2 percent from V1 to V4, with V2 the hardest single state to place. Errors concentrated on neighbouring levels: HR4 was read as HR3 on 27.8 percent of its trials and V1 as V2 on 29.9 percent, while confusion between non adjacent states stayed at or below 8.3 percent. No state fell below 49.3 percent. Identifying both channels correctly on the same trial, a harder sixteen-way judgement, reached 44.6 percent against a 6.25 percent chance level (95 percent CI 36.8 to 52.5), $t(35)=9.90$, $p<.001$. Table~\ref{tab:legibility-tests} lists all four chance-level tests, and Figure~\ref{fig:legibility} reports the open-phase and structured-phase recognition rates against chance together with the HR and HRV confusion matrices.

\centering
\caption{Tests of identification accuracy against chance. Each participant contributed one accuracy proportion per comparison, tested against the fixed chance level with a two-sided one-sample $t$ test ($df=35$). Cohen's $d_z$ is the standardized mean difference from chance (mean deviation divided by the within-participant SD of the accuracy proportion).}
\label{tab:legibility-tests}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}lcccccc@{}}
\toprule
Comparison & Mean accuracy & Chance level & 95\% CI & $t$ & $p$ & Cohen's $d_z$ \\
\midrule
Open-phase best match (5-option) & 46.5\% & 20\% & 37.0 to 56.0 & 5.66 & $<.001$ & 0.94 \\
HR identification (4-option) & 66.3\% & 25\% & 58.5 to 74.2 & 10.66 & $<.001$ & 1.78 \\
HRV identification (4-option) & 58.2\% & 25\% & 51.0 to 65.3 & 9.42 & $<.001$ & 1.57 \\
Joint HR+HRV identification (16-option) & 44.6\% & 6.25\% & 36.8 to 52.5 & 9.90 & $<.001$ & 1.65 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}

\begin{figure*}[t]

  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_4_Identification_Accuracy_Confusion.png}
  \caption{Identification accuracy and confusion patterns. (a) Unaided best-match accuracy for the four Phase A anchor conditions against the 20 percent chance level. (b) Mean participant accuracy for HR recognition, HRV recognition, and joint HR+HRV recognition in the structured phase, against the 25 percent and 6.25 percent chance levels. (c) HR confusion matrix and (d) HRV confusion matrix, showing the percentage of trials at each actual state that were assigned to each selected state.}

  \label{fig:legibility}

\subsection{Perceived Activation and Urgency (H2)}
The heart rate channel carried activation as intended. Each step up the heart rate state raised perceived urgency by 0.40 points on the seven point scale (95 percent CI 0.24 to 0.55), $t(35)=5.17$, $p<.001$, with condition means of 3.15, 3.51, 3.99, and 4.31 from HR1 to HR4. Rated arousal rose in step, by 0.41 points per level (95 percent CI 0.25 to 0.57), $t(35)=5.33$, $p<.001$, from 3.10 to 4.33. Both trends survived Holm correction and support H2, though the slope is markedly shallower than the accuracy results alone would suggest, indicating that participants graded urgency and arousal cautiously even where they identified the underlying state correctly. Figure~\ref{fig:mappings}(a) shows the two curves with between participant confidence intervals.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_5_Perceptual_Mappings.png}
  \caption{Validation of the intended perceptual mappings. (a) Perceived urgency and SAM arousal rise with heart rate state, from low activation (H1) to overdrive activation (H4). (b) Perceived stability and SAM dominance/control fall, while support intention rises, as ring coherence declines across the variability states, from high coherence (V1) to fragmented variability (V4).}
  \Description{Two line charts with confidence intervals: urgency and arousal rising across the four heart rate states, and stability and dominance falling while support intention rises across the four variability states.}
  \label{fig:mappings}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Perceived Stability and the Call for Support (H3 and H4)}
The variability channel carried regulation as intended. Each step toward a more fragmented ring lowered perceived stability by 0.46 points (95 percent CI 0.31 to 0.60), $t(35)=6.52$, $p<.001$, with means of 4.90, 4.48, 3.96, and 3.55 from V1 to V4, supporting H3. Over the same states the intention to offer support rose by 0.48 points per level (95 percent CI 0.36 to 0.61), $t(35)=7.78$, $p<.001$, from 3.30 to 4.74, supporting H4. Rated control moved on the same axis, falling by 0.51 points per level (95 percent CI 0.37 to 0.65), $t(35)=7.35$, $p<.001$. Table~\ref{tab:trend-tests} lists all five trend tests, and Figure~\ref{fig:mappings}(b) plots the variability panel.

\label{tab:trend-tests}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}llccccc@{}}
\toprule
Outcome & Predictor & Effect per level & 95\% CI & $t$ & Adjusted $p$ & Cohen's $d_z$ \\
\midrule
Perceived urgency (rises) & HR state & 0.40 & 0.24 to 0.55 & 5.17 & $<.001$ & 0.86 \\
SAM arousal (rises) & HR state & 0.41 & 0.25 to 0.57 & 5.33 & $<.001$ & 0.89 \\
Perceived stability (falls) & HRV fragmentation & 0.46 & 0.31 to 0.60 & 6.52 & $<.001$ & 1.09 \\
SAM dominance (falls) & HRV fragmentation & 0.51 & 0.37 to 0.65 & 7.35 & $<.001$ & 1.22 \\

\end{table*}

\subsection{Performance Across the Full Grid}
Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, legibility could have collapsed where an active pulse met a fragmented ring. It did not, though the grid was less uniform than the marginal HR and HRV accuracies alone suggest. Joint recognition held across all 16 combinations, with no cell falling anywhere near the 6.25 percent chance level. The hardest cells, tied at 33.3 percent, were H2V2 and H3V2: focused and high activation states each paired with stable, near-baseline variability, the two conditions where a mid-range pulse met the least visually distinctive ring pattern. The easiest cells, tied at 55.6 percent, were H1V1, H3V1, and H4V1, all pairing the most coherent ring with a clearly placed activation level; the one exception was H2V1, at 41.7 percent, indicating that a coherent ring alone did not guarantee an easy read when paired with the subtler H2 activation step. Even the hardest cells stayed more than five times above chance, which confirms that participants still read the two channels compositionally rather than by memorising sixteen unrelated icons, even though the compounding of two mid-range states measurably increased difficulty. Support intention rose consistently across the grid in the same direction as the marginal HRV trend, from a low of 3.00 at H1V1 to a high of 5.06 at H4V4, and rose within every HR row as variability fragmented. Figure~\ref{fig:conditions} shows both patterns across all 16 combinations.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_6_Full_Condition_Grid.png}
  \caption{Performance across the full HR $\times$ HRV condition grid. (a) Joint recognition accuracy for all 16 combinations, against a 6.25 percent chance level; the hardest cells paired stable variability with mid-range activation, and the easiest paired coherent variability with a clearly placed activation level. (b) Mean support-intention rating for the same 16 combinations, rising with both activation and fragmentation.}
  \Description{Two heatmaps of the four heart rate states by four variability states: joint recognition accuracy, with all cells well above chance and the lowest values concentrated among stable-variability, mid-activation combinations, and mean support-intention ratings, increasing toward the high-activation, fragmented-variability corner.}
  \label{fig:conditions}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Usability and Workload (H5)}
The display read clearly and cost a moderate but sub-midpoint amount of effort. Clarity averaged 5.22 of 7 and coordination usefulness 5.45, while the three Task Load Index items stayed below the scale midpoint, with mental demand at 3.64, effort at 3.47, and frustration at 2.53. Overall confidence was 5.81 and visual comfort 5.61. These results support H5, though workload sat closer to the middle of the scale than a minimal-effort display would, consistent with the more moderate recognition accuracy reported in Section~\ref{sec:study1-legibility}. Table~\ref{tab:usability-workload} reports each measure with its 95 percent confidence interval.

\toprule
Measure & Mean & 95\% CI \\
\midrule
Clarity & 5.22/7 & 5.08-5.35 \\
Coordination usefulness & 5.45/7 & 5.37-5.52 \\
Mental demand & 3.64/7 & 3.30-3.97 \\
Effort & 3.47/7 & 3.24-3.71 \\
Frustration & 2.53/7 & 2.21-2.85 \\

\end{table}

\subsection{How Participants Read the States}
The open responses explain the recognition scores. Participants reported reading the two channels together rather than in isolation, using pulse speed for activation and the ring for control, which is what the compositional design intended, and several described weighing one cue against the other before answering. As P12 put it, ``The combination worked better than either cue alone. Pulse speed gave the intensity, and the ring showed whether it was controlled.'' The pulsing centre and the ring's degree of fragmentation were named repeatedly as the features that carried the most meaning. The states participants found hardest were the adjacent middle levels on each scale, and the closest pair by a wide margin was the stable and reduced variability states, which matches the neighbour confusions concentrated in the recognition data; P4 described exactly this: ``V2 and V3 were the closest pair for me. I could see that the ring changed, but not always enough to choose confidently.''

\subsection{Usefulness, Boundaries, and Willingness to Use}
Participants judged the display useful for cooperative play, and several tied that judgement directly to context and control rather than to the visual design alone; P13 said the information ``could be genuinely helpful during difficult sections of a game. It becomes invasive only if teammates or spectators can access it automatically.'' The same responses set conditions. Participants asked for control over who can see their status, for the display to stay optional and mutable, and for consent to be built into the default rather than added later, and some described continuous live body data as inherently personal, with P4 willing to accept it ``only with clear consent and an easy off switch.'' Willingness to adopt was expressed most often for cooperative and squad modes where a teammate cannot pause to explain their state, as P2 put it: ``Probably yes. It could replace some voice-chat explanations during intense moments, as long as it stays optional.''

\section{Study 2 Methodology: Live Multiplayer Evaluation of Physiological Awareness Cues}
\label{sec:study2-methods}
\noindent
To evaluate whether the architecture proposed in Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods} supports coordination during actual cooperative play, we designed a second study that keeps Study 1's visual language untouched and moves the evaluation into live cooperative gameplay, asking whether live physiological visibility changes teammate awareness, support timing, coordination behavior, workload, and privacy perception, and whether the wearable-to-interface pipeline itself operates reliably enough for live play. This section reports Study 2's design, apparatus, and procedure in full; its results are reported in Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
Study 2 addressed four research questions: whether a live HR/HRV avatar cue improves remote teammates' awareness of one another during cooperative gameplay (RQ2.1); whether it improves support timing and coordination relative to no cue and to a numeric physiological display (RQ2.2); whether an ambient animated cue feels less invasive and more usable than explicit numeric disclosure (RQ2.3); and whether the implemented wearable-to-interface pipeline operates reliably enough for live cooperative play (RQ2.4).

We set six hypotheses, distinct from and building on the H1 to H5 of Study 1. \textbf{H2.1 (Awareness):} participants report higher teammate awareness in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition. \textbf{H2.2 (Coordination usefulness):} participants rate the Icon Cue condition as more useful for coordination than both the No Cue and Numeric Cue conditions. \textbf{H2.3 (Support response):} teams respond faster to scripted teammate-pressure events in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition. \textbf{H2.4 (Privacy/invasiveness):} the Numeric Cue condition is rated as more invasive than the Icon Cue condition. \textbf{H2.5 (Workload):} the Icon Cue condition does not produce higher workload than the Numeric Cue condition and remains at or below the midpoint of the workload scale. \textbf{H2.6 (Communication pattern):} the Icon Cue condition reduces general uncertainty and status-check communication while increasing targeted support behavior.

\subsection{Gameplay Task and Physiological Apparatus}
The gameplay task is modeled on Overcooked-style cooperative cooking, in which players deliver orders by collecting, preparing, and cooking ingredients, then plating and delivering the finished dish before time expires, splitting tasks on the fly and adapting roles under time pressure \cite{carroll_utility_2019, humancompatibleai_overcookedai}, a genre that has supported human-AI coordination research \cite{carroll_utility_2019} and multiplayer studies of role division and shared strategy \cite{baek_toward_2022}; Overcooked! 2 itself officially supports up to four simultaneous players \cite{team17_overcooked_2018}. We adapted this structure into a custom BioTerrarium-themed task rather than using copyrighted commercial levels, preserving the core action chain of ingredient collection, preparation, reactor cooking, plating, and delivery, so that team performance, role division, and moments of teammate overload remain measurable and comparable across conditions.

Study 2 implements the physiological pipeline that Study 1 left as a proposed architecture. Before gameplay, each participant wore a physiological wearable device and completed a three-minute seated baseline calibration to set personal resting HR and HRV. During gameplay, live HR and HRV values were classified relative to each participant's own baseline into the same four HR states and four HRV states validated in Study 1 (Tables~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} and~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification}), and a state change had to persist across consecutive display windows before the visible cue updated, to limit flicker. The system logged raw physiological values, personal baselines, classified and displayed HR/HRV states, timestamps, missing-data events, and sensor-to-interface latency, which together speak to RQ2.4.

\subsection{Participants and Team Size}
We recruited 36 adult participants, organized as 12 teams of three players in soft Prep, Cook, and Runner roles, rather than 9 teams of four, even though Overcooked! 2 officially supports four-player sessions \cite{team17_overcooked_2018}. Three-player teams were chosen because two-player designs already anchor the Overcooked coordination paradigm of task splitting and role adaptation \cite{carroll_utility_2019}, because team-size research finds that additional members increase coordination overhead \cite{hoegl_smaller_2005, straub_cost_2023} and a fourth player would double the pairwise communication channels relative to three, and because 12 teams provide more independent team-level units for the team-by-condition analyses below than 9 teams would. Roles were introduced during training but remained flexible: players could switch tasks mid-mission, logged as strategy-change events rather than protocol violations. Recruitment mirrored Study 1: participants were drawn from the author's university subject pool and personal network, took part voluntarily and without compensation, and all recruited participants completed all three mission blocks, so no team contributed incomplete data. Table~\ref{tab:s2-demographics} summarises the achieved sample.

\subsection{Interface Conditions and Counterbalancing}
Each team completed three seven-minute missions, one under each of three interface conditions: a No Cue baseline showing only the standard game interface; a Numeric Cue condition showing teammates' HR and HRV as explicit numerical or categorical values; and an Icon Cue condition showing the animated HR/HRV avatar cue validated in Study 1, in which heart rate controls pulse speed, glow, brightness, and halo expansion, and heart-rate variability controls ring coherence, segmentation, jitter, and stability. Table~\ref{tab:s2-design} summarizes the purpose of each condition and the comparisons and hypotheses it feeds. Condition order was counterbalanced with a Latin-square schedule across three groups of four teams, so that every condition appeared in every serial position, and the three mission layouts described below were rotated across condition orders so that no condition was systematically paired with an easier or harder layout; the full team-by-team schedule is reported in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Study 2 interface conditions, their purpose, and the comparisons and hypotheses each feeds.}
\label{tab:s2-design}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.14\textwidth}YYYp{0.14\textwidth}@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Condition} & \textbf{Interface shown} & \textbf{Purpose} & \textbf{Main comparison} & \textbf{Hypotheses} \\
\midrule
No Cue & Standard game interface; no physiological information of any kind. & Baseline coordination and communication without any bodily-state cue. & Reference level for both other conditions. & H2.1-H2.6 (baseline) \\
Numeric Cue & Teammates' HR and HRV shown as explicit numeric or categorical values. & Upper bound on explicit disclosure; isolates the value of cue \emph{content} from cue \emph{form}. & vs.\ No Cue (value of any cue); vs.\ Icon Cue (form) & H2.2, H2.4 \\
Icon Cue & Animated avatar halo validated in Study 1 (pulse/glow/expansion for HR; ring coherence/jitter for HRV); no numbers or emotion words. & Tests whether the ambiguous, label-free encoding supports coordination as well as explicit disclosure, at lower invasiveness. & vs.\ No Cue (value); vs.\ Numeric Cue (form) & H2.1-H2.6 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Mission Layouts and Scripted Pressure Events}
The three mission layouts reproduce common Overcooked-style cooperation patterns: a shared congestion kitchen with clustered resources and collision risk, a divided pass kitchen requiring mandatory item passing between zones, and a bottleneck-route kitchen where solo routing is inefficient and temporary support pays off. Each seven-minute mission also included three scripted pressure events, controlled coordination probes rather than random obstacles, each overloading a different role while the other two players had incomplete information about it (Table~\ref{tab:pressure-events}).

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Scripted teammate-pressure events used in every Study 2 mission. Each event overloads one role while the other two players hold incomplete information about it.}
\label{tab:pressure-events}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.20\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Event} & \textbf{Trigger} & \textbf{Overloaded role} & \textbf{Primary measure} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Ingredient Bottleneck} & Around 2:00, two active orders require the same ingredient type. & Prep & Support response time from event onset. \\
\textbf{Pot Overload} & Around 4:30, two reactor pots become active close together. & Cook & Recovery time and burned-dish prevention. \\
\textbf{Service Rush} & Near the final phase, completed dishes, dirty plates, and delivery tasks accumulate together. & Runner & Appropriate support actions within 10 seconds. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Procedure}
Each session opened with consent and a demographic questionnaire, followed by wearable setup, the three-minute baseline calibration, and a short tutorial covering movement, roles, support actions, and the interface for the first assigned condition. The team then completed three mission blocks, one per condition, each followed by a post-condition questionnaire; a short break between blocks allowed sensor reliability checks. The session closed with a final comparison questionnaire in which participants ranked the three conditions and answered open-ended questions about usefulness, invasiveness, trust, confusion, and the control they wanted over their own physiological visibility.

\subsection{Measures}
The primary behavioral outcome was support response time, the seconds from a pressure event's onset to the first relevant support action by another teammate, because it directly tests whether physiological awareness helps players notice and respond to teammate strain. Secondary behavioral measures, logged automatically by the game client, included orders delivered, mission score, late and wrong orders, burned dishes, player idle time, collision/blocking count, task contribution balance, strategy changes, appropriate and false support actions, and recovery time; full operational definitions and unit of analysis for each are given in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}). Team communication was recorded and coded post hoc for task instructions, help requests, support offers, status checks, physiological and numeric references, uncertainty statements, and strategy changes, to test whether the cue changes the balance between general uncertainty and targeted support (H2.6). After each condition, participants completed seven-point items on teammate awareness, coordination usefulness, and trust; the NASA Task Load Index for workload \cite{hart_development_1988}; the Self-Assessment Manikin for affective interpretation \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, as in Study 1; selected Game Experience Questionnaire dimensions for player experience \cite{ijsselsteijn_game_2013}; and custom items on comfort sharing physiological data, invasiveness, and desired visibility control. Related items were combined into composite constructs where they held together statistically; Section~\ref{sec:study2-results} reports each construct's reliability before using it. Full item wording and the complete data-logging schema are also reported in the supplementary materials.

\subsection{Analysis Plan}
Behavioral outcomes were analyzed at the team-by-condition level with repeated-measures ANOVA, with planned contrasts comparing the Icon Cue against the No Cue condition, the Icon Cue against the Numeric Cue condition, and the Numeric Cue against the No Cue condition; the primary planned contrast was Icon Cue versus No Cue on support response time. Questionnaire ratings were analyzed at the participant-by-condition level with repeated-measures ANOVA. Coded communication frequencies were compared across conditions at the team-by-condition level, and physiological and interface logs were summarized descriptively to report sensor latency, missing-data rate, and state-display agreement as evidence for RQ2.4.

\subsection{Ethics Note for Reviewers}
All participants gave informed consent before participation and were told that HR and HRV data were collected for research and interface-visualization purposes only, not for medical diagnosis or emotional classification, and that the displayed states were non-diagnostic and non-emotional. Participants were assigned anonymous identifiers, physiological data were stored separately from identifying information, and participants could withdraw or stop physiological sharing at any time without penalty. Reported results use aggregate statistics and interface-state summaries wherever possible.

\section{Study 2 Results}
\label{sec:study2-results}
Study 2 achieved its planned sample without attrition: all 36 recruited participants, organized as 12 teams of three, completed all three mission blocks under all three interface conditions, yielding 36 team-missions (12 teams $\times$ 3 conditions), 108 scripted pressure events (36 team-missions $\times$ 3 events), 864 physiological log entries, and 488 coded communication utterances. We report system reliability first (RQ2.4), then the primary behavioral outcome and its supporting behavioral measures (H2.3), then the questionnaire constructs bearing on teammate awareness and coordination usefulness (H2.1, H2.2), workload and affective response (H2.5), and privacy and invasiveness (H2.4), then coded communication behavior (H2.6), and close with participants' final rankings and qualitative accounts.

\subsection{Sample, Data Completeness, and System Reliability (RQ2.4)}
\label{sec:s2-reliability}
Table~\ref{tab:s2-demographics} (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) summarizes the achieved sample. RQ2.4 asked whether the implemented wearable-to-interface pipeline operates reliably enough for live play; Table~\ref{tab:s2-reliability} answers this directly. Sensor-to-interface latency rose modestly from No Cue, when the pipeline logged physiology in the background without rendering it, to the two cue conditions, reflecting the added cost of computing and rendering a display element, but even the slowest condition stayed well under a second and did not accumulate across the seven-minute mission (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}A). Missing-data episodes were rare and, if anything, slightly less frequent once a cue was displayed, and the average dropout, when one occurred, lasted well under half a second in every condition (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}B and C). Restricted to the two conditions that rendered a physiological state, the displayed HR/HRV state matched the classified state on over 96 percent of samples in both conditions, confirming that the persistence rule described in Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods} controlled flicker without decoupling the display from the underlying signal. Classified states also covered the intended range rather than collapsing onto one level, with most gameplay sitting in the moderate HR2/V3 band and enough excursions into the higher-activation and lower-coherence states to exercise the full classification (Table~\ref{tab:s2-reliability}). State-change rates were comparable across conditions (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}E); because No Cue still logged classified states in the background without displaying them, this indicates that a visible cue did not itself alter the underlying physiological volatility. Figure~\ref{fig:s2-timeline} shows one team's mission end to end, aligning the three scripted pressure events against a player's HR and HRV state trace and the resulting support actions, as a worked example of what the pipeline logs. Together, these results answer RQ2.4: the acquisition, classification, and rendering pipeline that Study 1 left unbuilt operated reliably across 12 teams and three interface conditions, with latency, missing-data, and state-agreement figures in a range a live cooperative session can absorb.

\subsection{Support Response Time and Behavioral Coordination (H2.3)}
\label{sec:s2-behavioral}
H2.3 predicted faster support responses to scripted pressure events under the Icon Cue than under the No Cue baseline; this was Study 2's primary planned contrast, and it was supported. Teams took 15.3 seconds (SD 2.3) to produce the first relevant support action after a pressure event's onset under No Cue, 12.5 seconds (SD 1.7) under Numeric Cue, and 11.4 seconds (SD 1.2) under Icon Cue, an omnibus effect of condition (Table~\ref{tab:s2-behavioral}; Figure~\ref{fig:s2-support-rt}). The planned contrast confirmed H2.3 directly: the Icon Cue produced support responses 3.90 seconds faster than No Cue, a large effect ($d_z=-1.46$; Table~\ref{tab:s2-contrasts}). The Numeric Cue was also faster than No Cue, and the Icon Cue was numerically, but not significantly, faster than the Numeric Cue: both cue conditions cut support latency roughly in half relative to baseline, and the ambient icon matched, without clearly exceeding, the speed of an explicit numeric readout.

This pattern held across all three scripted event types rather than concentrating in one (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}A): support response time fell similarly for the Ingredient Bottleneck (15.0s to 12.7s to 11.6s), Pot Overload (15.5s to 11.1s to 11.4s), and Service Rush (15.8s to 12.6s to 11.4s) across No Cue, Numeric Cue, and Icon Cue respectively, so the benefit of a visible cue was not an artifact of one pressure event or one overloaded role. Recovery time, the seconds from the first support action until the overloaded role's queue returned to normal, showed the same ordering (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}B; Table~\ref{tab:s2-behavioral}), with the Icon Cue significantly faster than No Cue ($d_z=-1.53$) but not reliably faster than Numeric Cue.

The behavioral benefit extended to whether support was well targeted, not merely faster. Teams logged more appropriate support actions under both cue conditions than under No Cue, while the count of false support actions, help offered where none was needed, did not differ across conditions (Table~\ref{tab:s2-behavioral}): the cues moved responses toward relevance rather than simply toward more activity. The rate at which a pressure event drew an appropriate response within the scripted window rose from 63.9 percent under No Cue to over 83 percent under either cue. Team-level mission outcomes moved together with these behavioral changes (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-performance}; Table~\ref{tab:s2-behavioral}): orders delivered, mission score, and appropriate support actions rose under both cues relative to No Cue, while idle time, collisions, and the combined error count fell. Logged strategy-change events did not differ reliably across conditions, a behavioral count distinct from the coded communication measure of the same name reported in Section~\ref{sec:s2-communication}.

One asymmetry qualifies this otherwise consistent pattern: on mission score, orders delivered, and error count, the Numeric Cue numerically outperformed the Icon Cue, and for mission score the difference reached significance ($d_z=-0.73$; Table~\ref{tab:s2-behavioral}). The Icon Cue matched or exceeded the Numeric Cue on the speed and targeting of support, the outcomes closest to the scripted pressure events, but a team with exact numbers in view still out-scored a team reading the ambiguous halo on the mission's aggregate scoring metric, a nuance Section~\ref{sec:discussion} returns to.

\subsection{Teammate Awareness and Coordination Usefulness (H2.1, H2.2)}
\label{sec:s2-awareness}
We combined related seven-point items into three constructs before testing H2.1 and H2.2 (Table~\ref{tab:s2-constructs}): teammate awareness (three items: aware a teammate was under pressure, aware a teammate needed support, noticed state changes; Cronbach's $\alpha=.67$), coordination usefulness (five items: interface helped understand teammates, helped the team coordinate, helped decide when to help, reduced uncertainty, helped recovery from difficult moments; $\alpha=.77$), and trust (three items: trusted the interface's information, cue matched the game situation, could use it without overthinking; $\alpha=.66$).

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\caption{Post-condition questionnaire constructs and internal-consistency reliability ($N=36$ participants, 1 to 7 scale).}
\label{tab:s2-constructs}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.1}
\begin{tabularx}{\columnwidth}{@{}Xccc@{}}
\toprule
Construct & Items & Scale & Cronbach's $\alpha$ \\
\midrule
Teammate awareness & 3 & 1 to 7 & 0.67 \\
Coordination usefulness & 5 & 1 to 7 & 0.77 \\
Trust & 3 & 1 to 7 & 0.66 \\
Workload (TLX, reported by item)$^{a}$ & 4 & 1 to 7 & 0.13 \\
Player experience (GEQ)$^{b}$ & 5 & 1 to 7 & 0.40 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\begin{flushleft}
\footnotesize $^{a}$ The four Task Load Index items did not cohere into a reliable index; Section~\ref{sec:s2-workload} and Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire} report them individually, as in Study 1. $^{b}$ Game Experience Questionnaire subscales are conceptually heterogeneous rather than a single trait; the composite is reported descriptively.
\end{flushleft}
\end{table}

H2.1 predicted higher teammate awareness under the Icon Cue than under No Cue. This was supported with a large effect: awareness rose from 4.21 (SD 0.47) under No Cue to 5.40 (SD 0.52) under Icon Cue (Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}), and the planned contrast confirmed the increase ($d_z=1.63$; Table~\ref{tab:s2-contrasts}). Awareness under the Icon Cue was statistically indistinguishable from awareness under the Numeric Cue (5.41; $d_z=-0.01$): the ambient, label-free halo made a teammate's state exactly as noticeable as an explicit numeric readout, without exposing the numbers themselves.

H2.2 predicted that the Icon Cue would be rated more useful for coordination than \emph{both} No Cue and Numeric Cue. The first half of this prediction held: coordination usefulness rose sharply from No Cue to Icon Cue (Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}), a large effect ($d_z=1.93$). The second half did not hold: the Numeric Cue was rated \emph{more} useful for coordination than the Icon Cue (5.49 vs.\ 5.27, $d_z=-0.41$; Table~\ref{tab:s2-contrasts}), the reverse of the predicted direction. H2.2 is therefore only partially supported: an ambient cue clearly beats no cue at all, but does not beat an explicit numeric one on rated coordination usefulness, a pattern that echoes the mission-score asymmetry in Section~\ref{sec:s2-behavioral} and that several participants explained directly as a trade-off between the icon's glanceability and the numbers' precision (Section~\ref{sec:s2-qualitative}). Trust followed the same shape (Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}), with the Icon Cue again ahead of No Cue but behind the Numeric Cue ($d_z=-0.56$). Figure~\ref{fig:s2-questionnaire} plots these and the remaining constructs; exact means, omnibus tests, and effect sizes for every construct are in Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}, and the three planned contrasts for awareness, coordination usefulness, and trust are in Table~\ref{tab:s2-contrasts}.
