  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_5_Pressure_Event_Breakdown_Clean.png}
  \caption{Support response time (A) and recovery time (B) by interface condition, broken down by the three scripted pressure-event types. The benefit of a visible cue held across all three events rather than concentrating in one.}
  \Description{Two panels of connected-dot plots, one for support response time and one for recovery time, each showing three lines (No Cue, Numeric Cue, Icon Cue) across the Ingredient Bottleneck, Pot Overload, and Service Rush pressure events; both cue conditions sit below the No Cue line at every event.}
  \label{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_6_Team_Performance_Outcomes.png}
  \caption{Team performance outcomes by interface condition: (A) orders delivered, (B) mission score, (C) error count (late + burned + wrong orders), (D) appropriate support actions. Gray lines show individual teams; black markers show condition means $\pm$ 95\% CI.}
  \Description{A four-panel figure of team-level performance measures across the three interface conditions, with orders delivered and mission score rising under both cue conditions relative to No Cue, error count falling, and appropriate support actions rising, most under the Icon Cue.}
  \label{fig:s2-performance}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Workload and Affective Response (H2.5)}
\label{sec:s2-workload}
H2.5 predicted that the Icon Cue would not raise workload above the Numeric Cue and would stay at or below the midpoint of the workload scale. The four NASA-TLX items we collected (mental demand, temporal demand, effort, frustration) did not cohere into a single reliable index (Cronbach's $\alpha=.13$; Table~\ref{tab:s2-constructs}), so, following Study 1's practice, we report and test them individually, alongside a purely descriptive four-item mean (Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}; Figure~\ref{fig:s2-questionnaire}). On every item, the Icon Cue's mean stayed at or below the 4.0 midpoint of the seven-point scale and did not differ significantly from the No Cue baseline (all Holm-adjusted $p>.09$). The Numeric Cue, by contrast, significantly raised mental demand, temporal demand, and effort relative to No Cue; frustration did not differ across any pair of conditions. The four-item descriptive mean makes the same point at a glance (Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}), with the Icon Cue significantly lower than the Numeric Cue ($d_z=-0.51$; Table~\ref{tab:s2-contrasts}) and not reliably different from No Cue. H2.5 is supported: reading the ambiguous halo cost measurably less deliberate effort than reading exact numbers, and did not measurably exceed the cost of playing with no physiological display at all.

Affective ratings on the Self-Assessment Manikin, collected as in Study 1, moved with the constructs above (Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}): valence and dominance/control both rose from No Cue, with the Numeric Cue again rated as giving players a stronger sense of control than the Icon Cue ($d_z=-0.46$), consistent with the coordination-usefulness asymmetry in Section~\ref{sec:s2-awareness}. Arousal rose modestly and identically under both cue conditions relative to No Cue and did not distinguish Numeric from Icon at all. The Game Experience Questionnaire composite (competence, flow, challenge, enjoyment, social connectedness; $\alpha=.40$, reported descriptively given that GEQ subscales are conceptually heterogeneous rather than a single trait) rose from No Cue to both cue conditions and did not differ between them: whichever form the cue took, players reported a more positive play experience than with no cue at all.

\subsection{Privacy and Invasiveness (H2.4)}
\label{sec:s2-privacy}
H2.4 predicted that the Numeric Cue would be rated more invasive than the Icon Cue. This was strongly supported. On the single-item invasiveness rating, ratings rose sharply from No Cue to Numeric Cue, with the Icon Cue in between (Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}; Figure~\ref{fig:s2-questionnaire}). The planned contrast confirmed H2.4 directly: the Icon Cue was rated significantly less invasive than the Numeric Cue ($d_z=-1.22$; Table~\ref{tab:s2-contrasts}), even though it was also rated more invasive than showing no physiological information at all ($d_z=1.03$): withholding numbers and emotion words did not make the icon invisible as a privacy concern, but it roughly halved the perceived cost of the Numeric Cue's exact disclosure. Comfort sharing physiological data showed the mirror-image pattern, with participants significantly more comfortable under the Icon Cue than the Numeric Cue ($d_z=0.44$). The desire for visibility control and for an immediate off switch rose sharply once any physiological data was shown, regardless of its form, and did not distinguish the Numeric from the Icon Cue: players wanted control over \emph{any} bodily disclosure, not only the more precise one. Finally, participants rated the Icon Cue as feeling useful without feeling diagnostic more strongly than either alternative, though the Icon-versus-Numeric difference on this item did not reach significance.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_7_Post_Condition_Questionnaire_Outcomes.png}
  \caption{Post-condition questionnaire outcomes by interface condition (participant-level means $\pm$ 95\% CI; light lines show individual participants). Panels C through F (trust, workload, invasiveness, player experience) illustrate the patterns reported in Sections~\ref{sec:s2-workload} and~\ref{sec:s2-privacy}; exact means and inferential tests for all constructs, including teammate awareness and coordination usefulness, are in Table~\ref{tab:s2-questionnaire}.}
  \Description{A six-panel figure of post-condition questionnaire ratings by interface condition, covering teammate awareness, coordination usefulness, trust, workload, invasiveness, and player experience; trust, player experience, and awareness rise under both cue conditions relative to No Cue, invasiveness rises most under Numeric Cue, and workload rises only under Numeric Cue.}
  \label{fig:s2-questionnaire}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Communication Behavior (H2.6)}
\label{sec:s2-communication}
H2.6 predicted that the Icon Cue would reduce general uncertainty and status-check communication while increasing targeted support behavior, relative to No Cue. Coders classified 488 utterances across the 36 team-missions into eight categories (Table~\ref{tab:s2-communication}; Figure~\ref{fig:s2-communication}). Uncertainty statements and help requests fell sharply once any cue was present ($F(2,22)=95.86$ and $66.00$ respectively, both $p<.001$), but the Icon Cue did not reduce uncertainty statements further than the Numeric Cue did ($p_{\text{Holm}}=.339$): both cues, not the icon specifically, cut general uncertainty talk. Status-check utterances, by contrast, did not differ reliably across conditions ($F(2,22)=1.37$, $p=.274$): naming a teammate's state ambiently did not measurably reduce how often players still asked each other directly how they were doing.

Support offers, the communication category closest to the study's primary behavioral outcome, rose specifically under the Icon Cue (Table~\ref{tab:s2-communication}), $F(2,22)=31.00$, $p<.001$, significantly ahead of both No Cue ($d_z=2.14$, $p_{\text{Holm}}<.001$) and, notably, the Numeric Cue as well ($d_z=2.35$, $p_{\text{Holm}}<.001$), which did not differ from No Cue. Strategy-change utterances followed the same icon-specific pattern ($F(2,22)=33.00$, $p<.001$). References to the displayed cue were, as expected, condition-locked: physiological-state references occurred only under the Icon Cue and numeric-value references only under the Numeric Cue, confirming that players talked about whichever representation they could see. Task-instruction utterances, the most frequent code overall, fell steadily as ambient awareness increased, consistent with less need to narrate the team's situation aloud once it was partly visible. H2.6 is therefore partially supported: both cues reduced general uncertainty and help-seeking relative to no cue, but only the Icon Cue specifically increased targeted support offers and strategy-change talk beyond what the Numeric Cue produced, the clearest communication-level signature of the ambient design.

\subsection{Final Preferences and Qualitative Findings}
\label{sec:s2-qualitative}
At the end of each session, participants ranked the three conditions on five criteria (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-preference}). The Icon Cue was named most useful for coordination by 50 percent of participants (Numeric Cue 39 percent, No Cue 11 percent) and preferred for real cooperative play by 53 percent (Numeric Cue 33 percent, No Cue 14 percent). The Numeric Cue was named easiest to understand slightly more often than the Icon Cue (47 percent vs.\ 36 percent, No Cue 17 percent) and most trustworthy slightly more often as well (47 percent vs.\ 39 percent, No Cue 14 percent), mirroring the coordination-usefulness and trust asymmetries reported in Section~\ref{sec:s2-awareness}. The No Cue condition was named least invasive by a wide majority (78 percent), with the Icon Cue a distant second (19 percent) and the Numeric Cue almost never chosen as least invasive, consistent with the invasiveness ratings in Section~\ref{sec:s2-privacy}.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_10_Final_Condition_Preference_Final.png}
  \caption{Participants' final condition preferences by criterion. Each row sums to 100 percent of participants' first-choice selections.}
  \Description{A stacked horizontal bar chart showing, for five criteria (most useful, easiest to understand, least invasive, most trustworthy, preferred for real play), the percentage of participants selecting No Cue, Numeric Cue, or Icon Cue as their first choice; the Icon Cue leads on usefulness and real-play preference, the Numeric Cue leads on ease of understanding and trustworthiness, and No Cue leads decisively on being least invasive.}
  \label{fig:s2-preference}
\end{figure*}

We coded the nine open-ended closing-interview questions using the theme labels applied during data collection (Table~\ref{tab:s2-qualitative}); each question was answered by all 36 participants. Participants frequently framed the Icon Cue and Numeric Cue as offering different things rather than one simply beating the other, a trade-off between glanceability and precision: ``The icon felt more like part of the game; the numbers felt like a measurement tool. The trade-off was speed versus precision'' (P042); ``They pointed in the same direction, but they did not feel interchangeable. I was more comfortable glancing at the icon'' (P012). Several participants described the cue changing how they interpreted a quiet teammate specifically, the scenario the design was built to address, and named timing as central, helping most in the brief window before a request would otherwise have been made and distracting most when read while moving through a busy screen (Table~\ref{tab:s2-qualitative}, Awareness and Workload/confusion rows), an account consistent with the workload pattern in Section~\ref{sec:s2-workload}. Privacy surfaced unprompted and consistently, with participants asking for sharing to be opt-in per match, an immediate off switch, and, in several cases, separate visibility permissions for the icon and the exact numeric values (Table~\ref{tab:s2-qualitative}, Privacy control row). These qualitative accounts converge with the quantitative results above: an ambient, label-free cue that a teammate reads as part of the game, rather than measures like a diagnostic panel, delivered most of the coordination benefit of explicit disclosure while asking less of players' attention and less of their privacy.

\FloatBarrier

\section{Discussion}
\label{sec:discussion}

Study 1 asked whether a teammate's body could be made readable at a distance without naming a feeling; Study 2 asked whether that readability changes what a team does together in live play. Both answer yes, largely in the predicted direction, with one recurring qualification that the closing subsection takes up.

\subsection{Legibility Without Emotional Labels, and as a Designed Resource}
The central Study 1 result is that an interface can withhold every emotion word and still be read, well above chance even if not to ceiling, with readings ordered by the underlying states across all five planned tests. This matters because it separates two things the shared physiology literature has tended to bundle: systems that name an emotion assume the naming is warranted \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, and systems that report warmer feeling between partners rarely check that the viewer can identify the state on show \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, hirsch_my_2023, hassan_augmenting_2024}. Prior ambiguous biosignal displays, closest among them Howell et al.'s skin-conductance work \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}, established that withholding a label does not prevent interpretation; our contribution is narrower and more quantitative, a chance-referenced identification rate for a label free display that is graded rather than categorical. That design choice was not incidental. Because measurement helps constitute what it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, because the categories an interface fixes carry consequences \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and because any reading of a body is situated \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, the interface offered graded cues to be interpreted in place rather than settled verdicts, and the qualitative responses show this reasoning reached participants, who valued that the display showed a pattern rather than a diagnosis. The theoretical contribution is therefore twofold: a method that turns abstract STS commitments into concrete visual choices, and evidence that the resulting artifact communicates, which moves the ambiguity argument from a critical stance into a validated design position \cite{howell_biosignals_2016, wakkary_things_2021, howell_tensions_2018}.

\subsection{Two Separable Physiological Channels}
Almost all prior work in this space transmits heart rate on its own. We encoded heart rate and heart rate variability as two visual channels, pulse and brightness against ring coherence, read as distinct social properties: activation drove urgency and arousal, while fragmentation lowered perceived stability and raised the intention to help. The two indices are physiologically distinct, since rate reflects momentary activation and variability reflects the autonomic regulation beneath it \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007, shaffer_overview_2017}, and the study shows this distinction survives translation into a single icon. Performance held across all sixteen combinations rather than collapsing where an urgent pulse met a broken ring, though joint accuracy dipped furthest where a mid-range pulse met the least visually distinctive, stable-variability ring, indicating that participants read the channels compositionally rather than memorising whole icons. This is evidence that more than one bodily signal can be carried at once, provided each is mapped to a visual variable a viewer can isolate.

\subsection{From Reading a State to Intending to Act}
A display meant for coordination has to do more than convey a state; it has to move a teammate toward a response. The variability channel did exactly that: as ring coherence broke down, the intention to offer support rose monotonically across the full condition grid. This links a physiological cue to a prosocial disposition rather than only felt closeness, and speaks to the coordination problem that motivates the work, where teammates who cannot read one another misjudge who needs help \cite{tan_role_2014, bisberg_communication_2025, leavitt_ping_2016}, extending the connection oriented framing of earlier heart rate sharing systems \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017, janssen_intimate_2010, jarvela_augmented_2021}. Study 2, below, tests whether that intention converts into faster support during live play.

\subsection{Design Implications and the Politics of Visibility}
Four implications follow for physiological interfaces in shared settings. Encoding relative to a personal baseline, rather than an absolute scale, kept states legible without asserting a clinical reading, a defensible default for social displays. Separable visual variables let a single icon carry two bodily signals, suggesting headroom for richer physiological communication than a single heart rate channel. Withholding emotion labels still produced a legible display and gained user acceptance, indicating that graded, open cues are a viable alternative to categorical affect display. Most consequential, control over visibility is a precondition rather than a refinement: participants accepted the display only when it remained optional, mutable, and subtle, and Study 2 confirmed why. Making a bodily signal visible changes the informational terms of play, and with them how blame, hesitation, and effort are read among teammates, precisely the redistribution of accountability that a politics of artifacts anticipates \cite{winner_artifacts_1980}. Participants in both studies asked for consent over who can see their status and for the ability to withdraw it, recasting privacy from an afterthought into part of the interface's core logic; a deployed version would need to treat the right to be illegible as seriously as the capacity to be read.

\subsection{From Legibility to Coordination}
Every claim above concerns whether the icon communicates, not whether communicating it changes what a team does together; Study 2 closed that gap by testing the Icon Cue against a No Cue baseline and an explicit Numeric Cue across scripted teammate-pressure events in live play. Teams supported a struggling teammate significantly faster under the Icon Cue than under No Cue, a large effect that held across all three pressure-event types, and logged more appropriate support actions without more false ones (Table~\ref{tab:s2-behavioral}): the graded legibility documented in Study 1 carries over into faster, better-targeted coordination, not only into correctly naming a state when asked.

The three-condition design also answered a comparison Study 1's single-arm design could not make: whether withholding numeric values costs or gains anything relative to explicit disclosure. The answer is not a clean win for ambiguity. On support response time, teammate awareness, and workload, the Icon Cue matched the Numeric Cue while costing significantly less in perceived invasiveness, the strongest form the ambiguity argument could take. But on coordination usefulness, trust, and mission score, the Numeric Cue was rated, and performed, significantly better (Table~\ref{tab:s2-contrasts}), a trade-off between glanceability and precision that participants named directly (Section~\ref{sec:s2-qualitative}): the icon was read faster and with less friction, but exact numbers gave players something concrete to plan around. A design wanting the numeric condition's edge on planning and trust without its privacy cost would need to combine the two, for instance keeping the ambient halo as the default and surfacing exact values on request, an implication Section~\ref{sec:limitations} returns to.

\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limitations}

Study 1 alone had no comparison condition and simulated cardiovascular states rather than a live wearable feed, so it could not establish that the label free design outperforms a simpler alternative or that a real wearable produces equally clean states under motion and noise. Study 2's three-condition design and live wearable pipeline (Table~\ref{tab:s2-reliability}) closed both gaps directly, and the comparison is mixed rather than a clean win for ambiguity, as Section~\ref{sec:discussion} details: readers should not treat Study 2 as settling the question in the icon's favor, only as showing it is competitive on the outcomes closest to the pressure events tested and behind on others. What remains open from Study 1 is accuracy: recognition was well above chance on every comparison but moderate rather than near ceiling, with the middle states on each channel reliably harder to separate than the endpoints (Table~\ref{tab:legibility-tests}), so a deployed system should budget for a comparable error rate, and Study 2 still relied on one vendor's sensing stack, so the baseline-relative classification's behavior on other consumer sensors, whose photoplethysmography accuracy and HRV estimation vary by device, remains untested; a deployed version also needs a fallback for players without a supported wearable, most simply a neutral, unanimated avatar.

Several limitations are specific to Study 2 or remain after it. Its scripted pressure events, while modeled on organic Overcooked-style bottlenecks, were researcher-triggered on a fixed schedule rather than emergent, so support response time measures reaction to a controlled probe rather than the full variety of overload moments a longer, unscripted session would produce; each team played each condition once, for seven minutes, which rules out unequal practice through counterbalancing but cannot speak to habituation, whether the icon's benefit holds, grows, or fades across dozens of sessions, or whether the Numeric Cue's edge on trust and coordination usefulness would persist as players learned to read the icon as fluently as they read numbers. Coded communication relied on a single primary coder per utterance rather than an independently double-coded reliability sample, so those results should be read as one systematic coding pass rather than inter-rater-validated categories. Both studies drew convenience samples from the author's university subject pool and personal network, voluntarily and without compensation, limiting generalization to a broader or more diverse population of remote cooperative-game players; both relied on self report, without a physiological ground truth for the viewer; and the bespoke BioTerrarium-themed task, built to avoid copyrighted commercial levels, leaves open whether the same pattern would hold in a shipped title with its own pacing and stakes. Finally, the privacy concerns Study 1 surfaced qualitatively, Study 2 confirmed quantitatively (Section~\ref{sec:s2-privacy}): a real deployment would still need to resolve consent, data ownership, and control over visibility, including the separate icon/numeric permissions several participants requested, before these findings could be carried into practice.
\section{Conclusion}
Distributed play removes the body from social interaction: teammates who would read one another's effort and strain at a glance in a shared room are left, across a network, inferring those states from text, voice, and avatar motion, and the resulting misreadings seed misplaced blame and broken coordination. This paper asked whether the missing bodily layer could be returned as a readable cue without collapsing it into a named emotion, and answered in two stages.

Study 1 showed that a label free display communicates: participants recovered heart rate and variability state from animation alone at 66.3 and 58.2 percent, well above chance, with readings ordered as designed across five trend tests and workload on the low side of the scale. Study 2 (Sections~\ref{sec:study2-methods} and~\ref{sec:study2-results}) carried that validated icon system into live cooperative play, testing it in 12 three-player teams against a no-cue baseline and an explicit numeric display. The graded legibility documented in Study 1 carried over into behavior: teams supported a struggling teammate 3.9 seconds faster under the Icon Cue than under No Cue, matching the Numeric Cue on speed and awareness while costing less in workload and invasiveness. The comparison was not a uniform win for ambiguity, however: the Numeric Cue was rated more useful for coordination and more trustworthy, and produced a higher mission score, a trade-off participants named directly in their own words and that qualifies, without overturning, the case for withholding numbers as a design default.

The contributions outlined in Section~\ref{sec:intro} stand as stated there. What Study 2 adds to them is a demonstration that a wearable driven, label free cue moves teammates to notice and support one another faster, not only to name a state correctly when asked, at a real but bounded cost in precision and trust relative to explicit disclosure. The boundaries participants placed on physiological visibility in Study 1 anticipated what Study 2 then measured directly: players in live cooperative play wanted the same consent, an immediate off switch, and, in several cases, separate visibility permissions for the icon and the exact numbers. Building that control into the interface's core, so that the right to be illegible is held as firmly as the capacity to be read, is the design priority a deployed version of this system would need to meet before the coordination benefit documented here could be carried into practice.
\section{Data and Materials Availability}
\label{sec:data-availability}
The anonymized trial-level dataset from Study 1 (participant demographics, Phase A and Phase B responses, and closing-questionnaire ratings), the stimulus animations, and the analysis scripts used to produce the statistics and figures in this paper will be deposited in a public repository upon publication. Study 2's materials, including the full post-condition questionnaire item bank, the communication-coding scheme, and the behavioral and physiological data-logging schema, together with its anonymized behavioral, physiological-logging, questionnaire, and communication-coding datasets and the analysis scripts used to produce the statistics, tables, and figures in Section~\ref{sec:study2-results}, will be deposited alongside them upon publication.

\section{Generative AI Use Disclosure}
A generative AI tool (ChatGPT) was used for language editing and restructuring of text drafted by the author across both studies reported in this paper; it was not used to generate the research idea, conceptual framework, study designs, stimuli, data, statistical results, or citations, and it played no role in participant recruitment, data collection, or analysis for either study. The author reviewed and takes responsibility for all content produced with its assistance.
